title: Forget Me Now

jokes.text: Bob Loblaw introduction — the attorney's name is itself a joke: 'Bob Loblaw' sounds like 'blah blah blah,' and his ad tagline 'Why should you go to jail for a crime someone else noticed?' is an absurdist non-sequitur
jokes.setup: A serious attorney ad introduces the new lawyer replacing Barry Zuckerkorn
jokes.punchline: 'Why should you go to jail for a crime someone else noticed?' — the tagline reframes guilt as purely a matter of detection, which is both legally absurd and darkly accurate
jokes.explanation: The name 'Bob Loblaw' is a masterpiece of dense wordplay — it sounds like 'blah blah blah' (empty legal talk), contains 'law' three times, and rewards phonetic attention. The ad tagline is a perfectly constructed absurdist joke: it sounds like a defense attorney's pitch but the logic is hilariously corrupt. Both land simultaneously. High originality, excellent economy.

jokes.text: 'Bob Loblaw, no habla Espanol' — the ad pivot to a Spanish-language disclaimer using the character's name
jokes.setup: Bob Loblaw's ad is wrapping up with standard legal disclaimers
jokes.punchline: 'Bob Loblaw, no habla Espanol' — the name phonetically sounds like 'no habla,' turning the disclaimer into a recursive joke using the lawyer's own name
jokes.explanation: Extends the 'Bob Loblaw' wordplay joke by finding a second phonetic dimension — the name also sounds like 'no habla.' This is layered, compact, and rewards careful listening. Genuinely impressive writing economy.

jokes.text: 'We're not here to talk nonsense to Bob Loblaw' — Michael's straight-man line that accidentally mirrors the 'blah blah blah' joke
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to be serious about finding out if the government has a case
jokes.punchline: His earnest line 'talk nonsense to Bob Loblaw' phonetically recreates 'blah blah blah' while meaning the opposite — he's rejecting nonsense
jokes.explanation: The triple-callback on the name continues to find new angles — now Michael's own dialogue recreates the phonetic joke without realizing it. This is the show's signature move: dense wordplay rewarded by attentive viewers. The rewatch bonus is high because you only catch it after the name has landed.

jokes.text: George Sr. through the surrogate Larry Mittleman threatens to spank Michael's ass raw, then Michael asks 'I'm sorry, have we met?' as if this is a stranger
jokes.setup: Michael is having a meeting without his father — then a random man in a hat starts threatening him with crude physical punishment
jokes.punchline: Michael's polite 'I'm sorry, have we met?' — as if his father threatening to spank him raw is normal and the issue is just social protocol
jokes.explanation: Michael's deadpan reaction cuts right to the heart of the absurdist premise — the comedy is in how he treats the procedural formality of introduction as the relevant problem, not the threat itself. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: George Sr. reveals the surrogate scheme: 'I hired this guy to wear a camera in his hat so he could be my eyes and ears while I'm stuck in this penthouse'
jokes.setup: Larry Mittleman is introduced as George Sr.'s 'surrogate' — a term that implies emotional or legal standing
jokes.punchline: The surrogate's actual function is revealed: a man wearing a hat-camera so George can spy remotely — deeply low-tech and petty given George's supposed power
jokes.explanation: The concept is brilliantly specific to George Sr.'s character: a man who ran a massive corporation now reduced to hat-cam surveillance. The mismatch between his grandiose self-image and petty spy scheme is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: George Sr. complaining about his ankle monitor: 'I can't even go in the hallway... without hearing... that beep, beep, beep'
jokes.setup: George Sr. is under house arrest in his penthouse and uses the surrogate to complain
jokes.punchline: His grievance is that the ankle monitor beeps when he tries to leave — the most minimal possible restriction on a man who should be in prison — delivered as though it's a major injustice
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy — George Sr.'s self-pity about house arrest in a penthouse is perfectly in-character. Not especially original but executed well.

jokes.text: Bob Loblaw says 'That's one of my partners. Excuse me.' and George Sr. responds 'I would like to be one of your partners.' The double meaning (business/romantic) goes over everyone's head
jokes.setup: Bob Loblaw excuses himself to take a call from a business partner
jokes.punchline: George Sr. expresses romantic interest in Bob Loblaw through the double meaning of 'partner,' which Bob apparently understands — 'He's running pretty fast. I think he did'
jokes.explanation: The rapid follow-up ('He's running pretty fast. I think he did') perfectly validates that the double meaning landed AND that Bob fled from it. The secondary beat about him 'flirting earlier' adds more texture. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: Lindsay says she's 'looking to get divorced' and Bob Loblaw immediately says 'Yes' before she even finishes — then they agree to 'call that a half hour'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is flirting with Bob Loblaw and mentions she wants to get divorced
jokes.punchline: Bob says 'Yes' instantly, billing the flirtatious exchange as a billable half-hour legal consultation
jokes.explanation: The 'call that a half hour' line is a neat double punchline — it reveals Bob is both interested and mercenary, billing the flirt session. Competent and funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Gob's dog-food joke: 'Better bring some dog food. 'Cause all the girls you date are dogs, and... Dogs love dog food, right, Pop?'
jokes.setup: Michael mentions he has a date with a beautiful woman named Rita
jokes.punchline: Gob's insult joke falls apart mid-delivery as he tries to salvage the 'dogs' metaphor with a factual statement about dogs eating dog food, then looks to his father for validation
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Gob's need for approval and his inability to land a joke without external validation. The crumbling mid-sentence recovery ('Dogs love dog food, right, Pop?') is exactly the kind of self-undermining that makes Gob so funny. High character integration.

jokes.text: Michael describes Rita as 'exquisitely beautiful, fun, carefree, smart, full of surprises' — then the narrator reveals she's secretly working with a suspicious British man who's dropping her off
jokes.setup: Michael gushes romantically about Rita's qualities to his skeptical family
jokes.punchline: The narrator immediately cuts to reveal she's being dropped off by a mysterious man warning her that the 'fun and carefree act' won't fool Michael forever — suggesting she has a secret
jokes.explanation: Classic AD structural joke: Michael's idealization is immediately undercut by the narrator with contradicting information. Rewatch bonus is very high because the 'secret' being revealed is that Rita has a developmental disability — making everything about this scene recontextualize completely.

jokes.text: 'The world's first analrapist' — Tobias reveals his combined job title as analyst and therapist
jokes.setup: Tobias is defending his credentials as a 'professional' man comparable to Bob Loblaw
jokes.punchline: 'An analyst and a therapist — the world's first analrapist' — the portmanteau is obscene and Tobias is completely unaware
jokes.explanation: One of the show's most iconic jokes. The portmanteau is obscene, the character is completely oblivious to it, and Lindsay's follow-up ('you were almost arrested for those business cards') confirms the joke was real-world-acknowledged within the fiction. Perfect character integration — only Tobias, with his combination of grandiosity, obliviousness, and latent sexuality, could deliver this. Maximum economy: four syllables contain the entire joke. Approaches the 9.5 anchor tier.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'You were almost arrested for those business cards.' Tobias: 'Yes, it did not look good on paper'
jokes.setup: Lindsay points out the business cards with 'analrapist' nearly got Tobias in legal trouble
jokes.punchline: 'It did not look good on paper' — Tobias's understatement is exquisitely calibrated; the word 'paper' doing double duty (literal business card paper / the written form of the obscene word)
jokes.explanation: The follow-up extends the analrapist joke perfectly. 'Did not look good on paper' is simultaneously an understatement, a pun on 'paper' (printed cards), and a demonstration of Tobias's continued obliviousness. Excellent economy and callback structure.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I stopped to raise our little daughter. But since we have both started to grow hair in unexpected places, I supposed I shall soon be working as a leading man.'
jokes.setup: Tobias is explaining why he stopped practicing as an analrapist
jokes.punchline: His reason for returning to work — that both he and his daughter are now growing hair — implies he's been waiting for puberty to pursue his acting career, while also being deeply odd
jokes.explanation: The joke works on Tobias's grandiosity (he's a 'leading man') and the bizarre comparative framing of his and Maeby's puberty. Slightly overlong but the character voice is very strong.

jokes.text: Tobias says 'I was hoping he would be gifted sexually' about Steve Holt dating his daughter Maeby
jokes.setup: Maeby is dating an older boy (Steve Holt) and the family is discussing expectations
jokes.punchline: Tobias, as a father, expresses hope that the boy his teenage daughter is dating is 'gifted sexually' — an absurdly inappropriate parental wish that reveals his complete social obliviousness
jokes.explanation: The joke turns on Tobias's spectacular failure as a parent — his concern isn't for his daughter's wellbeing but for her date's sexual performance. Perfectly in character for Tobias (latent homosexuality displaced onto his daughter's dating life). High character integration.

jokes.text: 'What a fun, sexy time for you' — Tobias's non-response to the news that older boys 'expect certain things' from his teenage daughter
jokes.setup: Lindsay notes that older boys expect 'certain things' from Maeby
jokes.punchline: Tobias's response is a cheerful, vacuous affirmation of his teenage daughter's implied sexual activity — betraying zero parental instinct
jokes.explanation: Five words that perfectly encapsulate Tobias's obliviousness and his failure as a parent. The sunny, spa-advertisement tone of 'fun, sexy time' applied to his minor daughter's sexual situation is deeply wrong in a hilarious way. High quotability — this line gets replicated constantly.

jokes.text: The 'shred the evidence' exchange: Bob Loblaw says it's illegal, Michael keeps interpreting legal speech as encouragement to shred, and Bob winks and says 'wink'
jokes.setup: Bob Loblaw warns them not to shred evidence — the prosecution needs it
jokes.punchline: Michael keeps responding 'So you're saying shred the evidence?' escalating through multiple denials until Bob literally says the word 'wink' out loud instead of winking — then George Sr.'s surrogate says 'He said that, too, Dad'
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is excellent — each denial builds anticipation until the absurdist peak: Bob says 'wink' instead of winking. The Larry/surrogate confirming 'He said that, too, Dad' is a perfect button. The joke has a clear three-beat escalation arc.

jokes.text: Steve Holt is revealed to be Gob's son — Gob denies it, George Sr. corrects him, and Michael and Maeby realize they've been dating their cousin
jokes.setup: Michael tells Gob to spend time with his 'real son' — Gob denies Steve Holt is his son
jokes.punchline: George Sr. confirms Steve Holt IS Gob's son ('That's my son, you pothead'), which triggers a rapid domino of realizations: Steve is Michael's nephew, Maeby's cousin, and the two teens have been dating their cousin
jokes.explanation: The rapid chain of recognition is beautifully structured — each character's 'wait' moment builds on the last. The dark twist (cousins dating) is perfectly Arrested Development. The setup has been built across multiple episodes, so the payoff feels earned.

jokes.text: Gob: 'At the kennel. Right. Sorry. You lobbed that one right over the plate, Michael. Home run.'
jokes.setup: Michael makes a weak comeback about Gob having a date 'at the kennel' (calling back the dog joke)
jokes.punchline: Gob self-commentates on the joke's quality using mixed sports metaphors ('lobbed right over the plate' / 'home run' — tennis/baseball mix), and apologizes for setting it up so easily
jokes.explanation: Gob's self-aware critique of the joke's structure ('you lobbed that one right over the plate') while using the wrong sport is perfectly in character — he's simultaneously too smart to miss it and too stupid to mix metaphors. The meta-commentary on joke quality is a signature AD move.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael hurried to the British section of Orange County for his date — only to find that Rita had chosen Wee Britain's one American-themed restaurant.' A British man shouts 'American! Get out of the road!'
jokes.setup: Michael goes to Wee Britain, the British enclave in Orange County, to meet Rita
jokes.punchline: Rita picks the American-themed restaurant inside the British neighborhood — the reversal of expectations (she's British, he's American, and she's picked the restaurant that's a caricature of his culture), plus a British man shouting at an American in the road
jokes.explanation: The structural irony is clear and well-executed. The rewatch value comes from understanding Rita's character — she chooses the American restaurant because she has limited understanding of social norms.

jokes.text: Rita says 'I've been having such fun on this space shuttle' while sitting in a prop space shuttle at the restaurant
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the American-themed restaurant to find Rita
jokes.punchline: Rita is sitting in a toy/prop space shuttle with childlike delight — presented as a normal date activity
jokes.explanation: The space shuttle moment works as a visual gag and character establishment. Rewatch value is significant — on first viewing it reads as quirky; after the reveal about Rita's condition it recontextualizes entirely. 'Strike one' from Michael's reaction beat is a clean button.

jokes.text: The American-themed restaurant in Wee Britain serves 64-ounce colas, doughnuts, and nachos simultaneously — 'to wash down this basket of doughnut and get your nachos started'
jokes.setup: Michael and Rita sit down at what looks like an Applebee's-style American restaurant in England's neighborhood
jokes.punchline: The menu represents the most grotesque American food excess possible — 64oz sodas, basket of doughnut (singular), nachos as a starter
jokes.explanation: The caricature of American dining excess is funny and the 'basket of doughnut' (singular) is a nice comic touch, but the bit is fairly conventional — British people mocking American food habits is well-trodden territory.

jokes.text: Michael says 'I guess this is what the British think of Americans — they like our food.' Rita responds 'Whatever do you mean?'
jokes.setup: Michael comments on the American-themed restaurant representing British perception of Americans
jokes.punchline: Rita's 'Whatever do you mean?' is either genuine confusion (because of her disability) or she genuinely likes it — both readings make her response funny for different reasons
jokes.explanation: Rita's response is funnier on rewatch when her disability is known — she genuinely doesn't understand the joke Michael is making about cultural perception. On first view it reads as naïve British charm; on rewatch it's poignant and funny.

jokes.text: Michael says 'your average American male is in a perpetual state of adolescence, you know — arrested development.' Rita: 'Hey, that's the name of the show.'
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining American cultural immaturity to Rita using psychological terms
jokes.punchline: Rita breaks the fourth wall by recognizing 'arrested development' as the show's title — an extremely self-aware moment
jokes.explanation: The show title drop is a well-executed meta joke. It works because it's earned through actual dialogue rather than forced — Michael genuinely uses the phrase in context. The callback to the literal show title is a satisfying piece of AD's signature self-awareness.

jokes.text: George Sr. (through Larry): 'If you like the small, why don't you come back for the medium?' — heard as Michael and Rita walk by
jokes.setup: Michael is on his date and the surrogate Larry appears unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: George Sr.'s non sequitur sales pitch ('if you like the small, come back for the medium') makes no contextual sense but is delivered through Larry as if completely normal
jokes.explanation: George Sr. attempting to interfere with Michael's date through the surrogate is funny in concept but this particular line is slightly weak — the content doesn't fully land. The absurdism of 'small/medium' isn't developed enough.

jokes.text: Michael sees his mother appearing and immediately tells Rita he doesn't have a family: 'I don't have a family. I can't believe I've never told anybody that before.'
jokes.setup: Michael has been trying to avoid his family meeting Rita; his mother appears
jokes.punchline: Michael's solution is to lie about having a family — phrased as if this is a brilliant lifehack ('I can't believe I've never told anybody that before') while he is, of course, the opposite of a man without family
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Michael's self-congratulatory tone about a pathetically obvious lie — 'I can't believe I've never told anybody that before' reads as genuine discovery. This is perfectly in character for Michael's self-delusion about being the normal one.

jokes.text: Michael catches Gob shredding the evidence boxes and says 'Gob, you can't shred this evidence. You'll end up in jail.' Gob: 'Dad told me to. How can I say no to that face?'
jokes.setup: Michael is cleaning the house and discovers Gob is shredding legal evidence
jokes.punchline: Gob's defense — 'Dad told me to. How can I say no to that face?' — is a non-sequitur because the 'face' he's talking about is the camera in Larry's hat, not an actual face
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: Gob's inability to defy authority (even in hat-camera form) and the absurdism of calling a hat-camera 'that face.' Good character work.

jokes.text: The army ceremony: 'Private Ashford, step forward... in acceptance of this medal, you proudly waive all rights to private citizenship and hereby reenlist.' 'If you agree with this, say thank you.' 'Thank you. Wow.'
jokes.setup: Buster is receiving a medal for having his hand eaten by a seal — a heroic ceremony
jokes.punchline: The acceptance of the medal is actually a re-enlistment trap — 'if you agree with this, say thank you' is buried in rapid legalese, and Buster says thank you enthusiastically before he understands
jokes.explanation: The trap is set up perfectly — the military ceremony lulls you into thinking it's a straight heroism beat, then the fine-print twist is revealed. 'If you agree with this, say thank you' is a beautifully constructed gotcha. Buster's 'Thank you. Wow.' captures his complete obliviousness. Well above average structurally.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'It's probably because a seal bit off his hand. The army has taken to giving medals for being food.'
jokes.setup: The family is confused about why Buster is getting a medal
jokes.punchline: The narrator's matter-of-fact explanation — 'the army has taken to giving medals for being food' — is a devastating deadpan reframe of military ceremony
jokes.explanation: Ten words that completely undercut military ceremony. 'Given medals for being food' is perfectly constructed — reframes Buster's accidental 'heroism' as passive victimhood with devastating economy. The narrator's detached tone makes it land harder. High quotability.

jokes.text: George Sr. has a revelation: 'It's so simple. My ankle monitor only goes off when I go out the front door.' He then immediately asks Larry to get balloons for a party.
jokes.setup: George Sr. has been frustrated by his ankle monitor all episode
jokes.punchline: His 'revelation' — that the monitor only triggers at the front door — is both a legitimate tactical insight AND immediately applied to the pettiest possible use: getting party balloons
jokes.explanation: This moment seeds George Sr.'s later balloon escape attempt — it pays off bigger later in the episode. On its own it's a solid character beat showing how George applies criminal resourcefulness to mundane party logistics.

jokes.text: Buster: 'Well, Mom's probably right. I couldn't even stand up to a seal.' Tobias: 'No, it wasn't the seal you couldn't stand up to — it was Lucille.' Buster: 'Oh, I'm getting chills.'
jokes.setup: Buster is doubting himself about accepting a military medal
jokes.punchline: Tobias's therapist-mode revelation — 'it wasn't the seal, it was Lucille' — is both a genuine psychological insight AND an obvious rhyme that makes it sound like a Dr. Seuss book
jokes.explanation: 'It wasn't the seal, it was Lucille' is a perfect couplet — it functions as a genuine psychological breakthrough and as an accidental Dr. Seuss rhyme. Buster getting chills at the revelation while we (the audience) laugh at the rhyme is excellent layered comedy. The brevity and rhyme structure give it high quotability.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'If this was a Lifetime Moment of Truth movie, this would be our act break. But it wasn't.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has just had a dramatic breakthrough moment with Buster
jokes.punchline: Tobias/narrator undercuts the emotional peak by pointing out it's not actually a Lifetime movie — and deflates the drama. Then immediately the actual act break happens anyway.
jokes.explanation: The meta joke about act breaks — then the irony that the real act break follows anyway — is a tight structural comedy move. It's self-aware without being smug. The Lifetime reference grounds the otherwise abstract meta humor in something specific and funny.

jokes.text: Lindsay on the phone with Bob Loblaw: 'The thing about Tobias is that he's never fulfilled me sexually.' Bob: 'Can you catalog for me the various ways you've been promised to be fulfilled whether or not said promise was made explicit?' Lindsay: 'You want me to be explicit?' Bob: 'Yes, but I will be needing to get off in four minutes.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is calling Bob Loblaw ostensibly for legal advice but is clearly flirting/pursuing him
jokes.punchline: 'I will be needing to get off in four minutes' — 'get off' means both ending the call and sexual release, and Bob appears oblivious to (or is deliberately exploiting) the double meaning
jokes.explanation: The whole exchange is a sustained double entendre — every legal phrase maps onto sexual language. 'Get off in four minutes' is the cleanest punchline, with 'Well, let's see if I can't hit that target for you' as a button. Well-structured sustained wordplay.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Well, let's see if I can't hit that target for you' — responding to Bob needing to 'get off in four minutes'
jokes.setup: Bob says he needs to 'get off' the phone in four minutes
jokes.punchline: Lindsay accepts this as a sexual challenge — 'let's see if I can't hit that target for you' continues the double entendre
jokes.explanation: A decent button to the phone call double entendre, but slightly weaker than the setup since the pattern has already been established. Still competent and character-specific.

jokes.text: Michael arrives home to find it covered in family photos and evidence; George Sr. is being 'surrogated' by Buster wearing a sealskin coat — 'by wearing her sealskin coat?'
jokes.setup: Lucille is teaching Buster to stand up to her by having someone else play her — via surrogating
jokes.punchline: The surrogate chosen to play Lucille is Buster wearing a sealskin coat — Buster dressed as his mother, in the skin of the creature that mutilated him
jokes.explanation: Layered character comedy: Buster dressed as his mother while wearing sealskin (the animal that bit him) is perfectly constructed. It calls back the surrogate concept, Buster's PTSD, and Lucille's controlling nature simultaneously. 'By wearing her sealskin coat?' — Michael's incredulous reaction beat is the button. High character integration.

jokes.text: Tobias, having tried on all of Lucille's clothes to play the surrogate: 'I thought the Donna Karan worked; you just didn't have the right shoes.'
jokes.setup: Tobias has apparently tried on multiple items of Lucille's wardrobe attempting to impersonate her
jokes.punchline: Tobias's critique isn't about whether wearing women's clothes is appropriate — it's about styling. He's entirely focused on the Donna Karan not working because of shoes.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Tobias's specific fashion expertise when the entire premise (a man trying on a woman's wardrobe to impersonate someone) should be the aberrant thing. His focus on the shoes is a perfect Tobias non-sequitur — he's oblivious to everything except fashion minutiae.

jokes.text: Maeby tries to cancel her date with Steve Holt: 'My whole family and I now have to go to my grandma's house 'cause my uncle lost his hand.' Steve: 'Awesome! So you and I can party alone at your house.'
jokes.setup: Maeby invents a family emergency to get out of her date with Steve
jokes.punchline: Steve Holt's response to 'my uncle lost his hand' is 'Awesome!' — either he's excited about Buster's military story or he's completely indifferent to the alleged tragedy
jokes.explanation: Steve Holt's 'Awesome!' is the perfect jock response — he hears 'lost his hand' and immediately pivots to opportunity. The joke also subverts Maeby's manipulative strategy (she's the smart one) by showing it backfires completely.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Maeby was too young to know what to do. Ironically, in her after-school job as a movie studio executive, she'd just green-lit two films that dealt with the very issue.'
jokes.setup: Maeby is struggling with her situation with Steve Holt and not knowing how to handle pressure to be sexual
jokes.punchline: As a teenage movie executive she's literally been greenlighting films about this topic while being personally clueless about how to handle the real-life situation
jokes.explanation: The irony is precisely constructed: Maeby has professional authority over coming-of-age narratives while being personally immature enough to need them. The narrator delivers the contradiction with perfect detachment. Strong character integration specific to Maeby's unique situation.

jokes.text: George Michael tells Michael 'I'm doing your cousin tonight' — Michael says 'So are you.' George Michael: 'What?' Michael: 'She's your cousin, too.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has been nursing a secret crush on Maeby (his cousin) and is about to tell Michael about Steve Holt and Maeby
jokes.punchline: George Michael's phrasing ('I'm doing your cousin') prompts Michael's immediate 'So are you' — a moment of shared wrongness before the realization lands
jokes.explanation: The exchange is dense with the cousin-dating running gag. 'So are you' lands as a genuine response — both boys are into their cousin (different cousins, same wrongness). The economy of the exchange is excellent.

jokes.text: Buster: 'No, he was just analraping me.' Lindsay: 'Then Michael barged in and kicked us out so he could shred all the evidence.' Lucille: 'Shred the evidence?'
jokes.setup: The family is discussing what happened at the penthouse while Tobias was 'surrogating' Lucille
jokes.punchline: Buster casually uses 'analraping' as a verb in a sentence — completely unfazed — then Lindsay misreports the evidence situation to create a new misunderstanding
jokes.explanation: Buster deploying 'analraping' as casual vocabulary is a perfect callback — it shows the word has entered the family's lexicon, which is both horrifying and funny. Lindsay's misreport sets up the subsequent plot twist efficiently.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, in our defense, Mom, we're not exactly dog people.' — responding to the idea that they haven't been nice to Michael's dates
jokes.setup: Lucille acknowledges the family hasn't been kind to Michael's girlfriends
jokes.punchline: Gob's defense ('we're not exactly dog people') subtly calls back his earlier 'dog' insult about Michael's dates, now used as an excuse for rudeness
jokes.explanation: The callback to the dog joke is economical and works on two levels: the literal meaning ('we don't like dogs') and the subtext ('we called his dates dogs'). Efficient use of earlier material.

jokes.text: Gob, planning the party: 'We need some weather balloons and maybe a tank of hydrogen. You know, really float those suckers.'
jokes.setup: Gob is planning a Welcome Rita party and discussing balloon supplies
jokes.punchline: His suggestion of hydrogen balloons is treated as practical party planning — but hydrogen is explosive, which pays off in George Sr.'s escape attempt later
jokes.explanation: The joke plants the seed for the balloon explosion at the end — on first view it's a Gob being stupid moment, on rewatch you see it's carefully constructed foreshadowing. The rewatch bonus is high.

jokes.text: The family picking up Rita in a stair car — the only vehicle 'big enough to hold them all' — and persuading her to come by saying 'We're nice'
jokes.setup: The family wants to pick up Rita and surprise Michael
jokes.punchline: They arrive in the Bluth stair car — a recurring absurd vehicle — and Rita's natural suspicion is met only with 'We're nice' as persuasion
jokes.explanation: The stair car is a long-running visual gag in the series. The scene's funniest element is the family's unsophisticated kidnapping technique ('We're nice') which completely fails on a normal adult but works on Rita for character-specific reasons.

jokes.text: Rita tries to leave; Lucille commands Buster to stop her; Buster sees the seal on Rita's backpack and tackles her, knocking her out
jokes.setup: Buster is ordered to stop Rita from leaving; he's been working all episode on overcoming his fear of seals
jokes.punchline: Buster overcomes his seal phobia by attacking the seal on Rita's backpack — knocking out an innocent woman in the process. 'Buster had overcome his fear of seals. Unfortunately, he'd done so by knocking out Michael's new girlfriend.'
jokes.explanation: The payoff is structurally perfect: the entire seal-phobia arc, built across the episode (and the series), resolves at the exact wrong moment in the most catastrophic possible way. The narrator's dry delivery ('Unfortunately, he'd done so by knocking out Michael's new girlfriend') is pitch-perfect. High character integration — only Buster's specific phobia/fixation could produce this result.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Buster had overcome his fear of seals. Unfortunately, he'd done so by knocking out Michael's new girlfriend.'
jokes.setup: Buster has had an emotional breakthrough by confronting a seal (on a backpack).
jokes.punchline: The narrator celebrates the victory then immediately undercuts it with the collateral damage — the therapeutic win created a criminal assault.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's delivery of the 'unfortunately' clause is the show at its best — the formal structure (achievement → devastating caveat) deployed for maximum comedic efficiency. 'Knocking out Michael's new girlfriend' as the means of therapeutic breakthrough is absurdist disaster comedy done right.

jokes.text: After knocking out Rita: 'You made a mistake. You're human... except for the hand.' / 'And soft. Soft and alive.' / 'All right, let's not add that to the charges.'
jokes.setup: The family has just knocked out Michael's girlfriend and is assessing the damage
jokes.punchline: Multiple rapid-fire comedy beats: the 'except for the hand' qualifier, Buster feeling up an unconscious woman ('soft and alive'), and Lucille's lawyer-mode 'let's not add that to the charges'
jokes.explanation: Three separate excellent jokes stacked in rapid succession. 'Except for the hand' is a perfect dark callback; 'soft and alive' is deeply wrong in a perfectly Buster way; 'let's not add that to the charges' is Lucille's legal awareness applied to assault. Each joke is distinct and lands separately.

jokes.text: Gob introduces the Forget-Me-Now: 'They're pills that create a sort of temporary forgettingness. So if somebody finds out how you do a trick, you just give 'em one of these. It's a mainstay of the magician's toolkit, like how clowns always have a rag soaked in ether.'
jokes.setup: The family needs to make Rita forget what happened; Gob proposes a Forget-Me-Now
jokes.punchline: Gob's explanation of the roofie as a magician's tool is absurdist in the best way — and the comparison to clowns with ether rags is a perfectly specific absurd detail that escalates the wrongness
jokes.explanation: The ether-soaked clown rag is one of the episode's best lines — it's specific, absurd, and adds a new dimension of wrongness to the joke. The framing ('mainstay of the magician's toolkit') treats roofying as legitimate professional practice. This is Gob's character at its finest — amoral obliviousness dressed in professional jargon.

jokes.text: Michael identifies the pill: 'Gob, this is Flunitrazepam. It's a roofie. Those are illegal.' Gob: 'Shut up, Mom. Don't make me give you another one of these.'
jokes.setup: Michael identifies the Forget-Me-Now as Rohypnol
jokes.punchline: Gob calls Michael 'Mom' (confusing him with Lucille) AND threatens to roofie his brother — then the narrator implies someone has already been given one
jokes.explanation: Calling Michael 'Mom' reveals Gob hasn't been paying attention and/or views authority figures interchangeably. The threat to roofie a family member — said casually — is perfectly dark. 'Don't make me give you another one' implies Gob has already roofied someone in the room.

jokes.text: 'Lindsay, help me! Three!' — presumably Lucille being roofied while calling for help
jokes.setup: Gob threatens to give someone another Forget-Me-Now
jokes.punchline: Lucille cries out 'Lindsay, help me! Three!' — the 'Three!' implies she's being given a third roofie, meaning she's been roofied multiple times already
jokes.explanation: The 'Three!' is a brilliantly compressed joke — one word telling us Lucille has already been roofied twice, both of which happened off-screen. Maximum economy. The callback structure mirrors the 'Snip, snap!' escalation pattern.

jokes.text: Steve Holt arrives to break up with Maeby and finds the romantic setup she's made — 'Well, this sends a mixed message.' He then says 'I'm going to go get sexy.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has set up a romantic situation and then Steve arrives planning to break up with her
jokes.punchline: Steve's 'Well, this sends a mixed message' is followed by him immediately reversing course — 'I'm going to go get sexy' — showing zero depth of conviction
jokes.explanation: Steve Holt's character is perfectly consistent — his surface-level 'mixed message' observation demonstrates actual self-awareness, then 'I'm going to go get sexy' immediately abandons any principle. Good character economy.

jokes.text: Michael calls in: 'Hi, it's Michael. I will be coming to the party because I love you, too.' The banner 'Welcome Rita' is being taken down and they have to make a new one.
jokes.setup: The party banner switches from 'Welcome Buster' to 'Welcome Rita' — now Michael calls to say he's coming
jokes.punchline: The banner has to change again immediately — a running gag of constantly revised party decorations tracking the family's mercurial intentions
jokes.explanation: The banner-changing gag is a functional running joke tracking the family's chaos. Not exceptional on its own but serves the escalating absurdity well.

jokes.text: Michael calls the answering machine to tell his family he loves them, only for Gob to be right there listening: 'Hi, it's Michael. I will be coming to the party because I love you, too.'
jokes.setup: Michael calls the house thinking no one is there, leaving a sincere message.
jokes.punchline: Gob is standing right there, hearing Michael's sincere declaration of love in real-time — a comically anti-climactic intimacy.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the accidental intimacy — Michael trying to leave a private message while Gob is present. The awkward in-person delivery of what was supposed to be a safe answering machine declaration is a solid situational bit.

jokes.text: Michael brings Rita to the party. She sees the family. Everyone freezes. Narrator: 'Michael feared he'd been caught in a lie about his family. His family feared they'd been caught by a woman they clubbed, drugged and left on a bench. It was awkward.'
jokes.setup: Rita arrives at the party and both parties freeze — each side has something to hide
jokes.punchline: The narrator's dual explanation — Michael's social embarrassment vs. the family's actual criminal conspiracy — culminates in the magnificent understatement 'It was awkward'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The narrator's parallel construction perfectly juxtaposes Michael's petty social anxiety against the family's literal assault charge. The payoff 'It was awkward' is a devastating understatement that earns the big laugh. Structurally flawless — two setups converge at the exact same moment. Approaches the 8+ anchor comfortably.

jokes.text: The banner is revealed: 'Family Love...' — apparently the rest is cut off or implies something inappropriate
jokes.setup: Michael sees the party banner the family made for him
jokes.punchline: 'Family Love...' — the banner is incomplete or truncated in a way that makes 'family love' sound incestuous given the cousin-dating subplots running through the episode
jokes.explanation: The banner's truncated message ('Family Love...') resonates differently given the entire episode's cousin-dating subplot. On first view it's a visual non-sequitur; in context it's a pointed meta-joke about the show's recurring incest themes.

jokes.text: Michael confesses he lied to Rita: 'I was embarrassed by them.' Then the family's reaction: 'Everybody's favorite son. Mr. Terrible.'
jokes.setup: Michael confesses he lied to Rita about having no family; the family hears this
jokes.punchline: The 'Everybody's favorite son / Mr. Terrible' reversal from the 'Mr. Wonderful' greeting earlier — showing the family's immediate pivot from support to condemnation
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'Everybody's favorite son' to 'Mr. Terrible' mirrors the earlier greeting almost word-for-word, which makes it a satisfying structural callback. The family's rapid reversal is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'And her, too good for us? Huh! Buster, you should have finished the job.' Buster: 'Job? What job?' Lucille: 'Did I say job? I meant Gob.' Then Gob outs Buster for the hitting and himself for the roofie.
jokes.setup: Lucille is covering up the assault on Rita; she accidentally implies Buster should have done more
jokes.punchline: 'Did I say job? I meant Gob' — a desperate word-swap cover that falls apart immediately as Gob volunteers all the information anyway
jokes.explanation: The Gob/job rhyme cover is economical and funny — then Gob immediately undermines the cover by volunteering everything anyway. The escalation of the confession ('Buster's the one that hit her! I just gave her the roofie.') is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: 'Everybody's favorite son.' / 'Mr. Terrible.' — The crowd's cheering slogan for Michael immediately inverted when Rita leaves.
jokes.setup: The family previously chanted 'Everybody's favorite son! Mr. Wonderful!' for Michael.
jokes.punchline: With Rita storming out, the same crowd immediately inverts: 'Mr. Terrible.' The slogan reverses in real time based on the group's reading of the situation.
jokes.explanation: The immediate 180-degree reversal — 'Mr. Wonderful' to 'Mr. Terrible' in the same breath — is a great structural joke. The crowd's fickleness and the Bluth family's complete lack of loyalty are both characterized simultaneously. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Gob tries to give Rita a Forget-Me-Now: 'Take this and love us again. No, just take it!' while Michael fights him off. Rita: 'Um, I'm leaving, too.'
jokes.setup: The situation is deteriorating; Gob decides more roofies are the solution
jokes.punchline: Gob physically trying to force a roofie on Rita while announcing 'Take this and love us again' — the most unhinged possible response — while Rita calmly says 'Um, I'm leaving, too'
jokes.explanation: 'Take this and love us again' is a great line — desperation framed as a reasonable request. Rita's 'Um, I'm leaving, too' is the perfect underreaction to someone trying to drug her in public.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Well, no good deed goes unpunished.' — after his attempt to roofie Rita fails
jokes.setup: Gob's plan to make Rita forget being assaulted backfires
jokes.punchline: Gob applies the 'no good deed goes unpunished' maxim to his attempt to roofie a woman — completely inverting the concept of a 'good deed'
jokes.explanation: The misapplication of a cliché is perfectly in character for Gob. He genuinely believes roofying someone to cover an assault is a 'good deed.' The economy is perfect — five words containing a massive character statement.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I'm getting rid of this thing [medal]. It has caused me nothing but pride and self-respect!' — then throws the medal away
jokes.setup: Buster has decided to reject the army re-enlistment; he blames the medal
jokes.punchline: 'It has caused me nothing but pride and self-respect!' — Buster treating positive emotions as negative outcomes, wanting to discard them because they're foreign to him
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best lines. The inversion — rejecting pride and self-respect as if they're curses — is perfectly calibrated to Buster's character (a man so dominated by his mother that independence feels dangerous). The delivery as a complaint about positive emotions is both sad and hilarious. High quotability, strong character integration, excellent economy.

jokes.text: Buster: 'I'm getting rid of this thing. It has caused me nothing but pride and self-respect!'
jokes.setup: Buster decides to get rid of the medal after learning it reenlisted him in the army.
jokes.punchline: 'It has caused me nothing but pride and self-respect' — Buster is discarding something because it made him feel good, which he treats as a problem. It's a perfect inversion of the expected complaint.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's second-best single line. The inversion of the expected complaint — you'd expect 'caused me nothing but trouble,' and instead get 'nothing but pride and self-respect' — is perfectly constructed. It simultaneously characterizes Buster (so starved of positive self-regard that pride and self-respect feel threatening and wrong) while being a shocking structural subversion. Only Buster's character makes this joke possible. The delivery of a positive outcome as a grievance is conceptually brilliant. Easily justifies 9+.

jokes.text: George Sr. makes his escape through the back (avoiding the front door monitor) using the hydrogen balloons as a hot-air balloon escape
jokes.setup: George Sr. discovered the ankle monitor only triggers at the front door; Gob arranged hydrogen balloons for the party
jokes.punchline: George Sr. uses the hydrogen balloons as a balloon escape vehicle — then the narrator: 'But it turns out that hydrogen is more explosive than helium.'
jokes.explanation: The payoff of two planted setups (hydrogen balloons + ankle monitor loophole) converging in an explosion is textbook Arrested Development: meticulous setup rewarded with maximum catastrophe. The narrator's 'but it turns out that hydrogen is more explosive than helium' is delivered as a basic physics fact, making it even funnier. Structurally exceptional — multiple threads converging.

jokes.text: George Sr.'s balloon escape — narrator: 'But it turns out that hydrogen is more explosive than helium.' — and the balloons explode.
jokes.setup: Gob earlier suggested hydrogen balloons for the party. George Sr. used them to try to float to freedom.
jokes.punchline: The hydrogen explodes, turning George Sr.'s escape attempt into a fireball — the narrator's flat scientific observation ('hydrogen is more explosive than helium') deadpanning the disaster.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to Gob's hydrogen balloon plant is perfectly executed. The narrator's scientific deadpan 'hydrogen is more explosive than helium' as the explanation for an explosion is a great button — it's technically accurate and completely inadequate as a response to someone blowing up. The callback architecture is satisfying.

jokes.text: George Michael comes home to find unconscious Steve Holt. Maeby: 'I gave him a roofie.' George Michael: 'He's your cousin.' Maeby: 'I should have told you that. Now you've drugged him and had your way with him.' George Michael: 'How is that even possible?'
jokes.setup: George Michael finds Steve Holt unconscious; Maeby confesses to roofying him
jokes.punchline: George Michael accuses Maeby of having 'had her way' with an unconscious man, and Maeby's 'How is that even possible?' deflects with the biologically incorrect angle while still admitting the drugging
jokes.explanation: The joke works on Maeby's pragmatic amorality — she roofied a boy not to sleep with him but to avoid sleeping with him, while also being impressed with herself for the solution. George Michael's confusion is well-placed.

jokes.text: George Michael comes home to find an unconscious Steve Holt: 'What have you guys been doing?' / Maeby: 'I gave him a roofie.' / 'A girl's got to grow up sometime.'
jokes.setup: Maeby was trying to avoid having sex with Steve Holt, and has apparently drugged him.
jokes.punchline: Maeby calmly reports she roofied Steve, then adds 'a girl's got to grow up sometime' — her version of 'growing up' being to drug her date.
jokes.explanation: 'A girl's got to grow up sometime' applied to administering a roofie is a great dark-comedy inversion of the coming-of-age cliché. Maeby's matter-of-fact delivery and George Michael's horror make perfect character foils. The Forget-Me-Now thread producing a second instance (this time done by a teenager to avoid sex) deepens the running gag.

jokes.text: Maeby: 'I just wanted him to think we did.' / 'Don't you see? I drugged him not to go all the way with him.' George Michael: 'Well, I think even the anti-drug people are going to be okay with that.'
jokes.setup: Maeby explains her reasoning for drugging Steve Holt
jokes.punchline: George Michael's 'I think even the anti-drug people are going to be okay with that' — a perfectly deadpan acknowledgment that this is a morally unusual but defensible use of rohypnol
jokes.explanation: George Michael's deadpan weighing of anti-drug sentiment vs. Maeby's specific use case is very funny — it applies bureaucratic ethical reasoning to an absurd situation. 'I think even the anti-drug people are going to be okay with that' is highly quotable and shows George Michael's nervous literal-mindedness.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Well, I think even the anti-drug people are going to be okay with that.'
jokes.setup: Maeby has explained she roofied Steve Holt specifically to avoid having sex with him.
jokes.punchline: Michael's response grants an implicit exemption from anti-drug policy for this specific use case — his pragmatism overriding normal parental outrage.
jokes.explanation: A gem of deadpan writing. 'Even the anti-drug people are going to be okay with that' is Michael's very specific, pragmatic endorsement of drug use for a teenager — presented with complete calm. The absurdity of invoking 'the anti-drug people' as an authority group and then granting them a hypothetical exemption is perfectly executed economy.

jokes.text: 'Well, let's get his pants back on.' / 'You took his pants off?' — Maeby reveals she removed Steve Holt's pants as part of the deception
jokes.setup: They're planning to clean up after the Steve Holt situation
jokes.punchline: Maeby casually mentions putting his pants back on, which reveals — to George Michael's horror — that she took them off in the first place
jokes.explanation: The pants reveal is a clean setup/punchline beat — the revelation is perfectly timed and George Michael's 'You took his pants off?' captures the audience's reaction. Good economy.

jokes.text: Lindsay's transcript with Bob Loblaw — the secretary transcribed their sexual phone call. Lindsay: 'I'm not paying for this.' Tobias: 'The thing you say on page seven looks like it could be worth some money.'
jokes.setup: Michael reports the retainer is exhausted because of Lindsay's calls to Bob Loblaw; turns out the secretary transcribed them
jokes.punchline: Tobias, reading the transcript of his wife's sexual phone call with another man, comments on page seven as potentially monetizable — completely unbothered by the content
jokes.explanation: Tobias's response to his wife's explicit phone transcript — focusing not on jealousy but on its potential commercial value — is perfectly in character. His marriage's dysfunction is illustrated in one line.

jokes.text: 'The thing you say on page seven looks like it could be worth some money.'
jokes.setup: Michael is reviewing the attorney transcript Lindsay was billed for.
jokes.punchline: Something Lindsay said to the attorney on page seven has legal/financial value — implying she inadvertently said something incriminating or exploitable — treated as a potential asset.
jokes.explanation: A functional throwaway joke about the legal phone call. The implication that Lindsay said something financially significant in a sex call is amusing but underdeveloped.

jokes.text: Michael admits to the family he was embarrassed by them and tried to impress Rita; Gob comforts him: 'She'd be lucky to have you, Michael. Sometimes you do surprise me.' Gob: 'I've got one more surprise for you.' Then begins singing 'It ain't easy being whi...'
jokes.setup: Gob has a genuine brotherly moment with Michael — he offers support and then promises one more surprise
jokes.punchline: The surprise is Gob beginning to sing a parody of 'It Ain't Easy Being Green' — but as 'It ain't easy being whi...' (implying white privilege) — cut off before finishing
jokes.explanation: The joke lands on two levels: Gob's self-pity (he's going to sing about his hard life as a white man) and the structural whiplash from genuine brotherly sentiment to Gob's narcissism. Cutting it off mid-word is perfectly timed — we get the joke without it being belabored.

jokes.text: Gob to Michael: 'She'd be lucky to have you, Michael. Sometimes you do surprise me. I've got one more surprise for you.'
jokes.setup: Gob has delivered a surprisingly sincere pep talk to Michael, and Michael thanks him warmly.
jokes.punchline: Gob pivots to 'I've got one more surprise for you' — the warmth of the sibling moment immediately transformed into Gob setting up a magic trick or something self-serving.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the whiplash from genuine brotherly warmth to Gob's inevitable self-interest. The setup earns emotional investment, which the punchline converts to comedy. Gob being incapable of a fully selfless moment is core character work.

jokes.text: Gob begins singing 'It ain't easy being whi...' and is cut off.
jokes.setup: Gob has set up a 'surprise' for Michael — the audience expects something meaningful.
jokes.punchline: The surprise is that Gob begins singing a parody of 'It Ain't Easy Being Green' but with 'whi[te]' — a deeply inappropriate race-based complaint, cut off mid-word by the scene change.
jokes.explanation: A masterpiece of comedic economy. The joke delivers maximum impact with minimal content: the cut at 'whi-' tells you everything while saying nothing explicit. Gob's specific kind of oblivious white privilege complaint, framed as a touching brotherly moment, is character-perfect. The incompleteness is the joke — and it's funnier for not finishing. Only Arrested Development cuts a joke this precisely. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Michael tells Rita everything: 'I'll start with the misdemeanors and then we're going to push right on through to the lighter treasons.' — then begins: 'So, 1983...'
jokes.setup: Michael decides to be fully honest with Rita about his family
jokes.punchline: 'Start with the misdemeanors and push through to the lighter treasons' — the categorization of his family's crimes by severity, with 'lighter treasons' as a legal category, implies there are also heavier treasons
jokes.explanation: 'Lighter treasons' is an excellent phrase — the qualifier 'lighter' implies an entire spectrum of treason in the Bluth family, which is both absurd and, within the show's logic, completely plausible. Good economy and very quotable.

jokes.text: Lindsay in a maid's uniform to 'work off her debt' with Bob Loblaw. Bob: 'Get a maid's uniform and come by my house around 7:00.' Narrator: 'There is no way to misinterpret that. Except the one.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay says she's willing to 'debase herself' to work off her legal debt; Bob tells her to come in a maid's uniform
jokes.punchline: Narrator: 'There is no way to misinterpret that. Except the one.' — implying Lindsay has misinterpreted a legitimate work request as a sexual invitation (or vice versa)
jokes.explanation: 'There is no way to misinterpret that. Except the one.' is a brilliant two-sentence joke — the first sentence sets up certainty, the second collapses it. The economy is exceptional (nine words for the whole joke). The ambiguity about which interpretation is correct makes it rewatchable. High quotability.

jokes.text: Steve Holt, traumatized, confronts Gob: 'I've made a huge mistake.' Gob: 'I know the feeling. I had you.' — then declares himself Steve's father
jokes.setup: Steve Holt, having been roofied, arrives to confront someone and says 'I've made a huge mistake'
jokes.punchline: Gob's response — 'I know the feeling. I had you.' — coldly confirms that Gob views fathering Steve as his own 'huge mistake,' then immediately pivots to claiming him
jokes.explanation: 'I know the feeling. I had you.' is one of the episode's best lines — a perfectly timed, devastating parental insult disguised as empathy. The rhythm is exceptional (four words / three words), the character integration is perfect (Gob is exactly this careless with his emotions), and the pivot immediately to 'I'm your father!' makes the whole thing funnier. Approaches the top tier.

jokes.text: Gob declares 'I'm your father, Steve Holt!' Steve: 'I won't forget this... Dad.' Gob, immediately: 'I will.'
jokes.setup: Steve Holt has a heartfelt moment accepting Gob as his father
jokes.punchline: Steve says 'I won't forget this, Dad' — clearly emotional. Gob's immediate 'I will.' punctures the moment completely, possibly also a reference to Forget-Me-Now pills
jokes.explanation: The final joke of the episode is a masterclass. Two words — 'I will.' — destroy a heartfelt father-son moment with maximum efficiency. It works on three levels simultaneously: (1) Gob's characteristic emotional avoidance, (2) possibly a literal reference to taking a Forget-Me-Now pill himself, (3) pure comedy timing — the brevity against Steve's emotional sincerity. This is as close to the 9.5 anchor as this episode gets. The rewatch bonus is significant because the Forget-Me-Now reading only lands on second viewing.

tmdb_overview: George Sr. is placed under house arrest by the family's new attorney Bob Loblaw, and finds that it's not exactly a walk in the park. Michael sets up his first date with Rita, but Rita has an unfortunate encounter with the family beforehand.
insight: Just-missed-elite threshold: 79.9 score driven by callback density in a forgetting-plot framework.
